audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a telephone conversation between a customer and a representative of an insurance company.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, motor insurance department.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'd like to ask about insurance for my car.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'll just take a few details.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. What's your name?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Patrick Jones.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The customer's name is Patrick Jones, so Patrick Jones has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, motor insurance department.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, hello, I'd like to ask about insurance for my car.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'll just take a few details.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. What's your name?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Patrick Jones.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And your address?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's 27 Bank Road.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: 27 Bank Road.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Is that in Greendale?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And what's your daytime phone number?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: My work number is 7 3 0 4 5 3.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And could I ask what your occupation is?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Dentist.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Now a few details about your car.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What size is the engine?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's 1200 ccs.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And the make and model?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's a Hewton Sable.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Could you spell the model name please?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, SABLE.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, uh, thanks, and when was it made?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Lovely.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I presume you've had a previous insurer.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We need to know the name of the company.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it was Northern Star.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And have you made any insurance claims in the last five years?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, uh, 1 in 1999.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And what was the problem?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It was stolen, but...
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's fine, Mr Jones.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's all we need to know at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And will there be any other named drivers?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Just the one.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And his name?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Simon Paynter.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Could you spell the surname please?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: PAYNTER.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK, thank you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And what relationship is he to you?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: He's my brother-in-law.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And what will you or Mr Paynter be using the car for?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, um mainly for social use.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Social use.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Will you be using it to travel to work?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, sometimes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Travel to work.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Anything else?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, that's it.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And finally, when would you like to start the insurance?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'll need it from the 31st of January.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Mr Jones, I'm getting a couple of quotes coming up on the computer now, and best bet looks like being with a company called Red Flag.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And that comes out at $450 per year.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, well, that seems OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Ha, it's quite a bit lower than I've been paying up to now.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Great, so would you like me to go ahead with that?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure, why not?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: How would you like to pay?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Transport and Attractions in the City

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a recorded message giving tourists' travel information in a large city.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 18.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 18.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Thank you for calling the tourist line.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are many different ways of getting round the city, and we'd like to suggest some you may not have thought of.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: How about a city trip by boat?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are four main stopping points, from west to east.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Stop A, Green Banks, stop B, City Bridge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Stop C, Roman Landing, and stop D, Newtown.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can find the main booking office at stop A.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The first boat leaves at 8 am, and the last one at 6:30 pm.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are also many attractions you can visit along the river.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At stop A, if you have time, you can visit the fine 16th century palace here, built for the king with its beautiful formal gardens.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's very near the booking office.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now you can enjoy every corner of this superb residence.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Stop B, why don't you visit Tower Restaurant with its wide range of refreshments?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is a place where you can sit and enjoy the wonderful views over the old commercial and banking center of the city.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Stop C, is the area where in the first century AD, invading soldiers crossed the river.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was much shallower than it is now.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's why this area is called Roman Landing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's an interactive museum to visit here, with a large shop, which has a good range of local history books.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At the furthest point of the trip, stop D, the most exciting place to visit is the new entertainment complex with seven-screen cinema, bowling alley, and video games arcade.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the message, you have some time to look at questions 19 and 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 19 and 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Besides the boat tours, there are city buses.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Two companies offer special services.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The top bus company runs all its tours with a live commentary in English.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Tours leave from 8:30 a.m. every 20 minutes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are departures from Central Station, Castle Hill and Long Walk.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is a hop-on hop-off service, and tickets are valid for 24 hours.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For further details call Top Bus on 02089447810. The Number One Sightseeing Tour is available with a commentary in eight languages.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Buses depart from Central Station every five to six minutes from about 9 a.m.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: With the last bus at around 7 p.m.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are also Number One services with an English-speaking...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: The Antarctic Centre and the Antarctic Treaty

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a seminar with Dr Martin Merryweather of the Antarctic Center in Christchurch, New Zealand.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: We're pleased to welcome Dr Martin Merryweather of the Antarctic Center in Christchurch, New Zealand, who has come along to talk to us today about the role of the center and the Antarctic Treaty.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Now my first question is about the choice of location for the center.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Why Christchurch?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Was it because of the climate?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Well, actually New Zealand is the second closest country to Antarctica.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: And Christchurch is often used on Antarctic expeditions.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: So it's because of where we are, coupled with our historical role.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: So tell us.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: What is the main purpose of the center?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Oh, well, we have two complementary roles.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: One is as a scientific base for expeditions and research, and the other is as an information center.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: Tell us something about the role as a scientific base.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Well, we're able to provide information about what scientists should take with them to the South Pole.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: For example, the center contains a clothing warehouse where expeditions are supplied with suitable clothing for the extreme conditions.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: I suppose you need a bit more than your normal winter coat.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Yes, exactly.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: And then there's also the specialist library and mapping services.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: Right, and which countries are actually located at the center?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Well, the center houses research programs for New Zealand, for the United States as well as for Italy.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: There's even a US Post Office at the American Air Force Base here.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: And what does the visitor center offer?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Well, since very few people will ever experience the Antarctic firsthand, the visitor center aims to recreate the atmosphere of Antarctica.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: There's a mock campsite where you can see inside an Antarctic tent and imagine yourself sleeping there.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: And the center also acts as a showcase for the unique international cooperation which exists in Antarctica today.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: What is it actually like at the South Pole?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: I know you've been there on a number of occasions.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Yes, I have, and each time I'm struck by the awesome beauty of the place.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: It's magnificent, but you can really only visit it in the summer months.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: October to March.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Yes, because it's completely dark for four months of the year.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: And in addition, it has to be the coldest place on earth.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: Uh. Colder than the North Pole.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Why is that?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Ah. Well, unlike the North Pole, which is actually a frozen sea, Antarctica is a land mass shaped like a dome, with the result that the winds blow down the slopes at speeds of up to 150 km an hour and that's what makes it so cold.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: Oh.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: And one other interesting thing is that Antarctica is the driest continent on earth, surprisingly.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: And so you have to drink large amounts of water when you're there.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: How old is Antarctica?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Oh, we're pretty sure it was part of a larger landmass, but it broke away from the rest of the continent 170 million years ago.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: How can you be certain of this?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Because fossils and rocks have been discovered in Antarctica, which are the same as those found in places such as Africa and Australia.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: Amazing to think that it was once attached to Africa.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the interview, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, let's just have a look at the Antarctic Treaty.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: How far back does the idea of an international treaty go?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Well, as far back as the 19th century.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: When 11 nations organized an international event.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: When was that exactly?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: In 1870 and it was called the Polar Research Meeting.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: And then, not long after that, they organized something called the First International Polar Year.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: And that took place when exactly?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Over two years from 1882 to 1883.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: But it wasn't until the 1950s that the idea of an international treaty was proposed, and in 1959 the treaty was actually signed.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: What do you see as the main achievements of the treaty?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Well, firstly, it means that the continent is reserved for peaceful use.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: That's Article 1, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: That's important since the territory belongs to everyone.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Yes, but not as important as Article 5, which prohibits any nuclear explosions or waste disposal.
audioscript.speaker: DOCTOR

audioscript.text: Which is marvelous.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Well, I'm afraid we're going to have to stop there, because I'm afraid we've run out of time.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Thanks for coming along today and telling us all about the center and its work.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 3.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear a seminar with Dr Martin Merryweather of the Antarctic Center in Christchurch, New Zealand.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 21 to 26.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: We're pleased to welcome Dr Martin Merryweather of the Antarctic Center in Christchurch, New Zealand, who has come along to talk to us today about the role of the center and the Antarctic Treaty.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Now my first question is about the choice of location for the center.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Why Christchurch?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Was it because of the climate?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Well, actually New Zealand is the second closest country to Antarctica.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: And Christchurch is often used on Antarctic expeditions.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: Right.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: So it's because of where we are, coupled with our historical role.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: So tell us.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: What is the main purpose of the center?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Oh, well, we have two complementary roles.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: One is as a scientific base for expeditions and research, and the other is as an information center.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: Tell us something about the role as a scientific base.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Well, we're able to provide information about what scientists should take with them to the South Pole.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: For example, the center contains a clothing warehouse where expeditions are supplied with suitable clothing for the extreme conditions.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: I suppose you need a bit more than your normal winter coat.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Yes, exactly.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: And then there's also the specialist library and mapping services.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: Right, and which countries are actually located at the center?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Well, the center houses research programs for New Zealand, for the United States as well as for Italy.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: There's even a US Post Office at the American Air Force Base here.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: Really?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: And what does the visitor center offer?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Well, since very few people will ever experience the Antarctic firsthand, the visitor center aims to recreate the atmosphere of Antarctica.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: There's a mock campsite where you can see inside an Antarctic tent and imagine yourself sleeping there.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: And the center also acts as a showcase for the unique international cooperation which exists in Antarctica today.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: What is it actually like at the South Pole?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: I know you've been there on a number of occasions.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Yes, I have, and each time I'm struck by the awesome beauty of the place.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: It's magnificent, but you can really only visit it in the summer months.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: October to March.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Yes, because it's completely dark for four months of the year.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: And in addition, it has to be the coldest place on earth.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: Uh. Colder than the North Pole.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Why is that?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Ah. Well, unlike the North Pole, which is actually a frozen sea, Antarctica is a land mass shaped like a dome, with the result that the winds blow down the slopes at speeds of up to 150 km an hour and that's what makes it so cold.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: Oh.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: And one other interesting thing is that Antarctica is the driest continent on earth, surprisingly.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: And so you have to drink large amounts of water when you're there.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: How old is Antarctica?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Oh, we're pretty sure it was part of a larger landmass, but it broke away from the rest of the continent 170 million years ago.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: How can you be certain of this?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Because fossils and rocks have been discovered in Antarctica, which are the same as those found in places such as Africa and Australia.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: Amazing to think that it was once attached to Africa.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the interview, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now, let's just have a look at the Antarctic Treaty.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: How far back does the idea of an international treaty go?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Well, as far back as the 19th century.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: When 11 nations organized an international event.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: When was that exactly?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: In 1870 and it was called the Polar Research Meeting.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: And then, not long after that, they organized something called the First International Polar Year.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: And that took place when exactly?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Over two years from 1882 to 1883.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: But it wasn't until the 1950s that the idea of an international treaty was proposed, and in 1959 the treaty was actually signed.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: What do you see as the main achievements of the treaty?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Well, firstly, it means that the continent is reserved for peaceful use.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: That's Article 1, isn't it?
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Yes.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: That's important since the territory belongs to everyone.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Yes, but not as important as Article 5, which prohibits any nuclear explosions or waste disposal.
utterances.speaker: DOCTOR

utterances.text: Which is marvelous.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Well, I'm afraid we're going to have to stop there, because I'm afraid we've run out of time.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: Thanks for coming along today and telling us all about the center and its work.
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 3.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: The Antarctic Centre was established in Christchurch because
options: New Zealand is a member of the Antarctic Treaty.
Christchurch is geographically well positioned.
the climate of Christchurch is suitable.

items.title: One role of the Antarctic Centre is to
options: provide expeditions with suitable equipment.
provide researchers with financial assistance.
ensure that research is internationally relevant.

items.title: The purpose of the Visitors' Centre is to
options: provide accommodation.
run training sessions.
show people what Antarctica is like.

items.title: Dr Merrywhether says that Antarctica is
options: unlike any other country.
extremely beautiful.
too cold for tourists.

items.title: According to Dr Merrywhether, Antarctica is very cold because
options: of the shape of the continent.
it is surrounded by a frozen sea.
it is an extremely dry continent.

items.title: Dr Merrywhether thinks Antarctica was part of another continent because
options: he has done his own research in the area.
there is geological evidence of this.
it is very close to South America.

body.title: Which TWO achievements of the Antarctic Treaty are mentioned by the speakers?
options: no military use
animals protected
historic sites preserved
no nuclear testing
fishing rights protected

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear a presentation given by a sport science student on her project on left and right handedness in sport.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: My topic is handedness, whether in different sports, it is better to be left or right sided, or whether a more balanced approach is more successful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'm left-handed myself, and I actually didn't see any relevance to my own life when I happened to start reading an article by a sports psychologist called Peter Matthews.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He spent the first part of the article talking about handedness in music instead of sport, which I have to say almost put me off from reading further.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But what I soon became struck by, was the sheer volume of both observation and investigation he had done in many different sports.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And I felt persuaded that what he had to say would be of real interest.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I think Matthews' findings will be beneficial, not so much in helping sports people to work on their weaker side.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But more that they can help them identify the most suitable strategies to use in a given game, although most trainers know how important handedness is.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At present, they are rather reluctant to make use of the insights scientists like Matthews can give, which I think is rather shortsighted, because focusing on individual flexibility is only part of the story.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Anyway, back to the article.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Matthews found a German study, which looked at what he called 'mixed-handedness'.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. That is the capacity to use both left and right hands equally.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It looked at mixed-handedness in 40 musicians on a variety of instruments.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Researchers examined a number of variables, for example, type of instrument played, regularity of practice undertaken, and length of time playing instrument.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And found the following: keyboard players had high levels of mixed-handedness, whereas string players like cellists and violinists strongly favored one hand.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Also, those who started younger were more mixed-handed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Matthews also reports studies of handedness in apes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Apes get a large proportion of their food by fishing ants from ant hills.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The studies show that apes like humans show handedness, though for them right and left-handedness is about equal, whereas about 85% of humans are right-handed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Studies showed that apes consistently using the same hand fished out 30% more ants than those varying between the two.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Matthews started researching several different sports, and found different types of handedness in each.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. By the way, he uses handedness to refer to the dominant side for feet and eyes as well as hands.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Anyway, his team measured the hand, feet and eyes of 2,611 players,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: and found that there were really three main types of laterality: mixed - you work equally well on both sides - both hand and eye;
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: single - you tend to favor one side but both hand and eye favor the same side; and cross-laterality - a player's hands and eyes favor only one side but they are opposite sides.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's start with hockey.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Matthews found that it was best to be mixed-handed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is because a hockey stick must be deployed in two directions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It would be a drawback to have hand or eye favoring one side.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: An interesting finding is that mixed-handed hockey players were significantly more confident than their single-handed counterparts.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Things are slightly different in racket sports like tennis.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Here, the important thing is to have the dominant hand and eye on the one side.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This means that there is a bigger area of vision on the side where most of the action occurs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If a player is cross lateral, the racket is invisible from the dominant eye for much of the swing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It means that they can only make corrections much later, and often the damage has been done by then.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And moving to a rather different type of sport, which involves large but precise movements, gymnastics.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's been found that cross hand-eye favoring is best.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The predominant reason for this is because it aids balance, which is of course absolutely central to performance in this sport.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And moving to a rather different type of sport, which involves large but precise movements, gymnastics.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's been found that cross hand-eye favoring is best.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The predominant reason for this is because it aids balance, which is of course absolutely central to performance in this sport.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Anita first felt the Matthews article was of value when she realised
options: how it would help her difficulties with left-handedness.
the relevance of connections he made with music.
the impressive size of his research project.

items.title: Anita feels that the findings on handedness will be of value in
options: helping sportspeople identify their weaknesses.
aiding sportspeople as they plan tactics for each game.
developing suitable training programmes for sportspeople.

items.title: Anita feels that most sports coaches
options: know nothing about the influence of handedness.
focus on the wrong aspects of performance.
underestimate what science has to offer sport.

items.title: A German study showed there was greater 'mixed handedness' in musicians who
options: started playing instruments in early youth.
play a string instrument such as the violin.
practise a great deal on their instrument.

items.title: Studies on ape behaviour show that
options: apes which always use the same hand to get food are most successful.
apes have the same proportion of left- and right-handers as humans.
more apes are left-handed than right-handed.

body.title: Left and Right Handedness in Sport

items.title: Hockey

items: hockey stick has to be used in <input>
mixed-handed players found to be much more <input> than others

items: gives a larger relevant field of <input>
